items.mistake: Calling fit_transform on the entire dataset before train/test split
items.fix: Split first, then scaler.fit on X_train only and transform X_val/X_test with those parameters—or use Pipeline inside cross_val_score.
items.why: Holdout rows influence μ, σ, min, and max, leaking future information into preprocessing and biasing evaluation.
items.example: StandardScaler().fit_transform(np.vstack([X_train, X_test])) lets test spend levels shrink the training z-scores used for model selection.

items.mistake: Scaling every model by default, including pure tree ensembles
items.fix: Skip scalers for tree/boosting models on raw numerics unless a downstream linear head, embedding distance, or API contract requires bounded inputs.
items.why: Trees are scale-invariant on monotone transforms; extra scaling adds pipeline complexity without changing splits.
items.example: Wrapping RandomForest in StandardScaler before every experiment slows iteration and confuses reviewers who expect optional scaling for trees.

items.mistake: Using MinMaxScaler on heavy-tailed revenue without checking train outliers
items.fix: Plot training distributions; winsorize or log-transform long tails, or prefer StandardScaler / robust scalers when outliers are valid.
items.why: A single large train value becomes max and maps typical transactions into a narrow band near zero.
items.example: Train max revenue $2M from one enterprise deal maps a $50k SMB row to ~0.025 on MinMax while StandardScaler at least preserves rank in z-space.

items.mistake: Forgetting to transform production inference data with the training scaler
items.fix: Persist the fitted scaler (joblib/pickle) with the model; call transform on live rows, never refit on streaming batches for serving stats.
items.why: Serving-time refit changes the mapping whenever new data arrives, breaking reproducibility and A/B parity with training.
items.example: Retraining StandardScaler nightly on all live traffic drifts μ/σ so yesterday's and today's scores are incomparable.
